title: Event 1
content:
EVENT REPORT “FCAJ COMMUNITY DAY” - EVENT 1, MAY 23 ,2026 Event name: FCAJ Community Day
Main topics: AI, Amazon Quick, Amazon CloudFront, LLM, and Multi-Agent System
Learn how AI can work more effectively when proper context is provided. Explore current trends in Generative AI, AI Assistants, and Multi-Agent Systems. Understand the role of AWS services such as Amazon CloudFront, Amazon QuickSight/Quick Suite, and Amazon Bedrock in real-world use cases. Listen to practical experiences in building products during hackathons and turning ideas into demos within a short time. Gain a broader view of how AI, data, and cloud can be combined in enterprise environments. Speakers Tinh Truong - Speaker of Context Is Everything: Making AI Actually Work for You Anh Pham - Speaker of Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick Thinh Nguyen - Speaker of From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation Team VIB - Sharing the UTMorpho project from LotusHacks Duc Dao - Speaker of Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings Vy Lam - Speaker of Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System: Startup Credit Scoring Event Agenda Time Topic Speaker / Team Main Content 08:30 - 09:00 Check-in Organizer Settle into the seat at floor 26 and prepare for the event. 09:00 - 09:30 Context Is Everything: Making AI Actually Work for You Tinh Truong Shared the importance of context in AI, the Second AI Brain concept, and how students can start building products with AI. 09:30 - 09:45 Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick Anh Pham Introduced AI assistant capabilities for data exploration, insight analysis, workflow creation with natural language, collaborative spaces, and dashboard building from raw data. 09:45 - 10:25 From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation Thinh Nguyen Presented how Amazon CloudFront supports content delivery, cost optimization, security, reliability, and performance improvement. 10:25 - 10:55 36 hrs with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality Team VIB Shared the journey of joining LotusHacks, brainstorming ideas, defining the problem, building UTMorpho within 36 hours, product demo, and key lessons learned. 10:55 - 11:00 Break Organizer Short break between sessions. 11:00 - 11:30 Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings Duc Dao Explained how LLMs choose the next token, the misconception that Temperature = 0 guarantees determinism, and why LLM outputs may still vary. 11:30 - 12:00 Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System: The Case of Startup Credit Scoring Vy Lam Presented a Multi-Agent System approach for startup credit scoring, including Virtual Credit Committee, guardrails, compliance, and ROI. Key Highlights Context Is Everything: Making AI Actually Work for You AI does not depend only on prompts but also heavily depends on the context provided. Without proper context, AI may produce generic, inaccurate, or unsuitable results. The Second AI Brain concept describes how AI can better remember, organize, and use personalized information. Learners need to define problems clearly, provide background information, and evaluate AI-generated outputs. Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick AI Assistants can help users explore data and analyze insights faster. Users can create intelligent workflows using natural language without writing complex code. Collaborative spaces can turn individual insights into shared team knowledge. Creating dashboards and reports from raw data using natural language can reduce the time required for data analysis. From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation Amazon CloudFront helps deliver content from edge locations to users with lower latency. CloudFront can support many types of workloads, not only static websites. The service can help optimize cost, improve performance, and strengthen security. CloudFront also plays an important role in improving system reliability when serving users across different regions. 36 hrs with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality Team VIB shared the process of building UTMorpho within 36 hours. The team started from brainstorming, problem definition, solution shaping, and demo implementation. The hackathon experience showed the importance of teamwork, time management, and fast decision-making. The sharing helped participants understand how an initial idea can become a working prototype. Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings Temperature = 0 does not absolutely guarantee that an LLM will always return the same output. Inference processes, system optimizations, and token selection may still create small differences in results. This behavior can affect systems that require high consistency. When applying LLMs in real-world systems, testing, logging, and output control strategies are important. Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System: Startup Credit Scoring Startup credit scoring is complex because startup data is different from traditional financial data. A Multi-Agent System can simulate different roles in a decision-making process. The Virtual Credit Committee model helps organize evaluation from multiple perspectives. Guardrails, compliance, and ROI are important considerations when deploying AI in enterprise environments. What I Learned AI Thinking Understood that AI needs clear context to generate better results. Learned that prompts are only one part of the process; background data and problem description are also important. Realized that AI should not be used mechanically and still requires human review and evaluation. AWS Cloud Learned more about the role of Amazon CloudFront in content delivery, performance optimization, and security. Understood how AWS services can support data analysis, dashboard creation, and system deployment. Gained a broader view of combining AI with cloud services to solve real-world problems. Product Development Learned how a team can quickly develop a product in a hackathon environment. Understood that defining the right problem is as important as building the solution. Realized that teamwork, task division, and product demo skills are important in software development. Enterprise AI Systems Understood that LLMs may still be non-deterministic even when the configuration appears deterministic. Learned about Multi-Agent Systems and how multiple agents can collaborate to solve a complex problem. Realized that guardrails, compliance, and risk control are important when deploying AI at enterprise scale. Application to Work Apply clearer prompt-writing methods when using AI to support learning, documentation, and information analysis. Use AI to suggest insights, support dashboard creation, or assist with initial data processing tasks. Pay more attention to performance and security-related AWS services such as CloudFront when learning AWS. When building personal projects, start from problem definition, target users, and product value. When using AI in real systems, include result validation, logging, and scope limitation. Event Experience Joining this event gave me a more practical perspective on how AI and AWS Cloud are being applied in different fields. The sessions were not only theoretical but also included practical examples such as building a product during a hackathon, optimizing systems with CloudFront, and applying Multi-Agent Systems to credit scoring.
The session about context in AI helped me understand that using AI effectively requires clear information and a specific goal. The LLM session also helped me realize that modern AI systems still need careful control, especially in use cases that require accuracy and stability.
Networking and Discussion Had the opportunity to learn from speakers working in AI, Cloud, and Data fields. Gained more insight into career directions related to AI, cloud computing, and data analysis. Collected useful ideas that can be applied to learning, internship tasks, and personal projects. Key Takeaways Context is a key factor that helps AI generate relevant and valuable results. AWS Cloud provides many services that support infrastructure, content delivery, AI, and data analytics. It is important not only to learn individual tools but also to understand how services work together to solve real-world problems. When deploying AI in real environments, stability, security, risk control, and business value must be considered. The event gave me more motivation to continue learning AWS, AI, and building cloud-based applications in the future. Some Images from the Event## Some Images from the Event

title: Blog 1
content:
LOAD TESTING ON AWS: IT’S NOT JUST ABOUT WHETHER A WEBSITE WORKS, BUT WHETHER IT CAN HANDLE HEAVY TRAFFIC When building a website or application, many people focus only on whether it runs correctly, is accessible, or has any interface issues.
However, in real-world environments where multiple users access the system simultaneously, a more important question arises: Can the system remain stable when it receives hundreds or even thousands of requests at the same time?
This is why load testing should be performed before deploying an application to production.
To address this challenge, AWS provides Distributed Load Testing on AWS, a solution that simulates large volumes of traffic, performs distributed load tests, and allows users to monitor test results directly through AWS services.
KEY FEATURES:
• Easy Test Scenario Creation:
Users can create test scenarios to simulate numerous requests sent to websites, APIs, or applications that need performance testing.
• Container-Based Load Generation:
The solution uses Amazon ECS running on AWS Fargate to launch load-generating containers. This architecture enables the system to scale efficiently when larger volumes of traffic need to be simulated.
• Web-Based Management Interface:
Users can create, execute, and monitor load tests through an intuitive web interface instead of relying entirely on command-line tools.
• Integration with Multiple AWS Services:
The architecture combines services such as Amazon S3, Amazon CloudFront, Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, AWS Step Functions, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon ECS on AWS Fargate, and Amazon CloudWatch to build a complete and scalable load testing solution.
• Performance Monitoring and Analysis:
After each test, the solution stores logs, metrics, and test results, enabling users to analyze application performance, identify bottlenecks, and make informed optimization decisions.
CONCLUSION: What I find most valuable about Distributed Load Testing on AWS is its ability to evaluate an application’s performance before real users start generating production traffic.
A high-quality application should not only function correctly—it should also be able to answer important questions such as: How many concurrent users can the system handle? Does the API response time increase as the request volume grows? Which component becomes the performance bottleneck? Is it necessary to scale additional resources? Can the system remain stable during extended periods of heavy load?
In my opinion, this is an excellent topic for anyone learning AWS because it brings together several essential cloud concepts, including Containers, Serverless Computing, Monitoring, Performance Testing, and Cloud Architecture.
Through exploring this solution, I gained a better understanding that before deploying any application to production, performing load testing is essential to ensure system stability, reduce the risk of failures, and deliver a better experience for end users.
AWS Architecture Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/solutions/latest/distributed-load-testing-on-aws/architecture-overview.html

title: Blog 2
content:
AWS WAF: A SHIELD PROTECTING WEB APPLICATIONS FROM COMMON ATTACKS When deploying a website or application to the internet, many people often focus on optimizing performance, scaling the system, or improving the user experience.
However, an equally important factor is security.
In reality, web applications are constantly facing numerous threats such as SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Bot Traffic, or Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks. Without proper protection, these attacks can disrupt services, compromise data, and degrade the user experience.
To address this problem, AWS offers the AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) service, which protects web applications from common threats while allowing for flexible management and construction of security policies.
In practice, AWS WAF is often deployed with Application Load Balancer (ALB), Amazon CloudFront, API Gateway, and AWS Shield to build a multi-layered security system for applications.
KEY FEATURES:
• Protecting applications from common attacks:
AWS WAF can detect and block many common attack types such as SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Local File Inclusion, and other malicious requests before they reach the application.
• Create custom security rules:
Users can build custom rules based on IP address, country, HTTP header, URI, or many other conditions to meet the specific security requirements of each system.
• Integrate with multiple AWS services:
AWS WAF can work with Amazon CloudFront, Application Load Balancer (ALB), Amazon API Gateway, and AWS AppSync to protect websites, web applications, and APIs.
• Combine with AWS Shield for DDoS protection:
When deployed with AWS Shield, the system can mitigate the impact of large-scale DDoS attacks. AWS Shield is responsible for network layer protection, while AWS WAF inspects and blocks malicious requests at the application layer. • Traffic Monitoring and Analysis:
AWS WAF can integrate with Amazon CloudWatch to collect logs, monitor traffic, detect anomalies, and assist administrators in implementing appropriate solutions.
REAL-WORLD SCENARIO: Suppose an e-commerce website is running a large promotional campaign and has thousands of users accessing it simultaneously.
During this time, an attacker could send a large number of requests containing malicious SQL code or carry out DDoS attacks to disrupt the system.
The deployment architecture is as follows: User → AWS WAF → Application Load Balancer → EC2 Auto Scaling → Application Web Servers
In this architecture: + AWS WAF will inspect all requests sent to the system. + Requests containing malicious code or exhibiting unusual behavior will be blocked at the WAF layer. + Application Load Balancer distributes legitimate traffic to application servers. + Auto Scaling automatically scales the number of EC2 instances when traffic increases. + CloudWatch logs and monitors all system activity.
As a result, the application maintains high availability while protecting user data from common attacks.
CONCLUSION: What I find impressive about AWS WAF is that it not only functions as a typical web application firewall but can also be combined with many other AWS services to build a comprehensive security system.
In an architecture using AWS WAF in conjunction with Application Load Balancer and EC2 Auto Scaling, AWS WAF acts as the first line of defense, inspecting and filtering all requests before they enter the backend.
When combined with AWS Shield, CloudFront, and CloudWatch, the system can both defend against DDoS attacks and detect and prevent application-layer attacks.
In my opinion, this is a very worthwhile service to learn about when studying AWS because it directly relates to important areas such as Security, Networking, High Availability, and Cloud Architecture.
Through this article, I understand better that security needs to be implemented from the outset, rather than just dealing with incidents when they occur. A good system not only needs to operate stably but also ensure the safety of data and users.
Document link:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/.../develope.../waf-chapter.html
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/.../aws-waf-and-shield.html

title: Blog 3
content:
AWS CODEPIPELINE: AUTOMATE YOUR APPLICATION DEPLOYMENT PROCESS When developing applications, manual building and deployment often takes a lot of time and is prone to errors.
To address this, AWS offers the AWS CodePipeline service, which automates the entire process from code updates to application deployment.
AWS CodePipeline is a CI/CD service that allows you to build a pipeline to automatically build, test, and deploy applications quickly and efficiently.
HIGHLIGHTS: • Software development workflow automation: Whenever there is a change in the source code, the pipeline will automatically trigger the build and deployment process. • Integration with multiple AWS services: CodePipeline can be integrated with CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, S3, EC2, and many other services. • Supports multiple code sources: Sources can be obtained from GitHub, AWS CodeCommit, or other systems. • Easy pipeline monitoring: Users can observe each step in the pipeline and check for errors. • Accelerated development: Reduces deployment time and minimizes errors caused by manual operations.
CONCLUSION: What I find useful about AWS CodePipeline is that it automates the entire application development and deployment process without requiring much manual work.
A modern system needs not only to run stably but also to: • Fast and continuous deployment. • Minimize errors during deployment. • Easy process monitoring and management.
In my opinion, this is a very important service when learning AWS because it is directly related to DevOps and CI/CD.
Through this article, I understand better that automation is a crucial factor in optimizing the software development process.
Document link:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codepipeline/latest/userguide/welcome.html

title: Create DynamoDB Table
content:
In the Dental Clinic Management System, Amazon DynamoDB tables are not manually created on the AWS Console but are automatically initialized through the Spring Boot backend source code.
The backend uses the DynamoDbTableInitializer class to check and create the necessary tables when the application starts.
@Configuration public class DynamoDbTableInitializer { private final DynamoDbEnhancedClient enhancedClient; public DynamoDbTableInitializer(DynamoDbEnhancedClient enhancedClient) { this.enhancedClient = enhancedClient; } @PostConstruct public void createTables() { createTableIfNotExists("users", User.class); createTableIfNotExists("invoices", Invoice.class); createTableIfNotExists("feedbacks", Feedback.class); createTableIfNotExists("doctor_schedules", DoctorSchedule.class); createTableIfNotExists("doctors", Doctor.class); createTableIfNotExists("services", DentalService.class); createTableIfNotExists("consultations", Consultation.class); createTableIfNotExists("blogs", Blog.class); createTableIfNotExists("appointments", Appointment.class); createTableIfNotExists("categories", Category.class); createTableIfNotExists("specialties", Specialty.class); createTableIfNotExists("clinics", Clinic.class); } } When the Spring Boot backend runs, the @PostConstruct annotation will automatically call the createTables() method. This method checks and creates DynamoDB tables if they do not already exist.
The tables created automatically include:
Table Function users Save user account information invoices Save invoice information feedbacks Save patient reviews doctor_schedules Save doctor’s schedule doctors Save doctor information services Save dental service information consultations Save consultation requests blogs Save blog posts appointments Save appointment schedules categories Save blog categories specialties Save specialties clinics Save clinic information After the application starts successfully, access AWS Console → DynamoDB → Tables to check the tables that have been created.
If the tables show Active status, the DynamoDB table initialization process is complete.

title: Create EC2 Instance
content:
In this step, we will create an Amazon EC2 Instance to deploy the Spring Boot backend of the Dental Clinic Management System.
Accessing Amazon EC2 Log in to the AWS Management Console, search for EC2, and select the EC2 service.
In the left-hand menu, select Instances, then click Launch instances to begin creating a new server.
Configuring the Instance Name In the Name and tags section, enter the Instance name:
Dental-Backend This name makes it easy to identify the server used to deploy the system’s backend.
Select Operating System In the Application and OS Images (Amazon Machine Image) section, select:
Amazon Linux 2023 AMI This AMI provides a stable Linux environment for installing Docker and running backend applications.
Select Instance Type In the Instance type section, select:
t3.micro This instance type is suitable for practice and test deployment environments.
Select Key Pair In the Key pair (login) section, select the previously created Key Pair:
keydental This Key Pair is used to SSH connect to the EC2 Instance after the server is initialized.
Security Group Configuration In the Network settings section, create a new Security Group or use an existing one.
In this workshop, the Security Group is configured to open the necessary ports:
Type Port Purpose SSH 22 SSH connection to EC2 HTTP 80 Access application via HTTP Custom TCP 8080 Access Spring Boot Backend The source is configured as:
0.0.0.0/0 to allow internet access in the practice environment.
Initializing an EC2 Instance After completing the configuration, review the Summary section on the right side of the screen.
If the information is correct, click Launch instance to create the EC2 Instance.
Upon successful initialization, AWS will display the message Successfully initiated launch of instance.
Checking the Instance Return to the Instances page to check the status of the EC2 Instance.
When the Instance changes to Running and Status check is displayed successfully, the EC2 is ready to use.
After completing this step, we have successfully created an EC2 instance to deploy the system’s Spring Boot backend.

title: Create S3 Bucket
content:
In this step, we will create an Amazon S3 Bucket to store images for the Dental Clinic Management System.
Creating an S3 Bucket Log in to the AWS Management Console. Search for Amazon S3. Select Buckets. Select Create bucket. Configure the Bucket with the following information: Bucket name: dental-service-images-huy AWS Region: Asia Pacific (Singapore) - ap-southeast-1 Keep the default settings and select Create bucket. After successful creation, the Bucket will appear in the Amazon S3 Buckets list.
Checking the Bucket Select the dental-service-images-huy Bucket to check the stored objects.
In the Bucket, the system has organized folders to manage images by function:
avatars/: Stores avatar images.
clinics/: Stores clinic images.
services/: Stores service images.
Checking CloudFront After successful creation, the Distribution status will change to Enabled.
Copy the CloudFront Domain name for use in the Backend or Frontend when accessing images.
After completing this step, the system will use CloudFront to distribute images from Amazon S3, speeding up loading and improving access performance. After completing this step, Amazon S3 is ready for the Backend to store and retrieve system images.

title: Introduction
content:
System Introduction The Dental Clinic Management System is a cloud-based web application designed to streamline dental clinic operations and provide patients with a convenient online appointment booking experience. The system enables patients to register, log in, browse available dental services, view dentist information, schedule appointments, and track appointment status. In addition, administrators can efficiently manage dentists, services, patients, and appointments through a centralized management portal.
The application is developed using a 3-Tier Architecture, separating the Presentation Layer (Frontend), Application Layer (Backend), and Data Layer (Database). This architecture improves scalability, maintainability, security, and overall system performance while making it easier to manage and expand future functionalities.
Workshop Overview This workshop demonstrates how to deploy the Dental Clinic Management System on Amazon Web Services (AWS) using multiple AWS services to build a secure, scalable, and highly available cloud-native application.
The workshop covers the deployment and configuration of the following AWS services:
AWS Amplify is used to host and deploy the ReactJS frontend application. Amazon CloudFront delivers static content globally with low latency, while Amazon Route 53 manages DNS and domain routing. AWS WAF protects the application from common web attacks such as SQL Injection (SQLi) and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). Amazon EC2 hosts the Spring Boot backend application and processes business logic through RESTful APIs. Application Load Balancer (ALB) distributes incoming traffic to improve application availability and scalability. Amazon DynamoDB stores application data, including user accounts, dentists, services, and appointment information. Amazon S3 stores static assets, uploaded images, and supporting documents. AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) manages authentication and authorization for AWS resources. AWS Secrets Manager securely stores sensitive credentials and application secrets. AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) encrypts sensitive data and manages encryption keys. Amazon CloudWatch monitors system performance, collects logs, and provides operational insights. Amazon SNS delivers system notifications, while Amazon SES sends appointment confirmation and notification emails to users. By following this workshop, readers will learn how to prepare the AWS environment, deploy both frontend and backend applications, configure database and storage services, implement security best practices, monitor system performance, and perform resource cleanup after deployment.
System Flow Users/Doctors access the website via a domain managed by Amazon Route 53.
Amazon Route 53 redirects requests to Amazon CloudFront to optimize access speed and content delivery.
AWS WAF inspects and filters requests before they reach CloudFront to prevent attacks such as SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), and unusual requests.
Amazon CloudFront distributes the ReactJS interface deployed on AWS Amplify and static resources hosted on Amazon S3.
When users perform functions such as logging in, scheduling appointments, or managing data, Frontend (AWS Amplify) sends API requests to the Application Load Balancer (ALB).
The Application Load Balancer (ALB) forwards requests to the Amazon EC2 server running the Spring Boot Backend application.
Amazon EC2 handles system business processes such as user authentication, doctor management, services, appointments, and other functions.
During processing, Amazon EC2 stores and retrieves images from Amazon S3 and uses Amazon SES and Amazon SNS to send emails and notifications as needed.
Finally, Amazon EC2 reads and writes data to Amazon DynamoDB. Amazon CloudWatch monitors system activity and sends alerts via Amazon SNS when it detects problems or exceeds configured thresholds.

title: Verify Email
content:
In this section, we will configure the Amazon Simple Notification Service (SNS) to send notifications from the Dental Clinic Management System via an SNS Topic.
1. Creating an SNS Topic Log in to the AWS Management Console, search for Amazon SNS, and select Topics.
Select Create topic and configure the following information:
Type: Standard Name: dental-clinic-notification Keep the default settings and select Create topic.
2. Creating a Subscription After the Topic is created, register an email address to receive notifications.
Open the newly created Topic. Select Create subscription. Under Protocol, select Email. Under Endpoint, enter the email address that will receive notifications. Select Create subscription. 3. Confirm Subscription Amazon SNS will send a confirmation email to your registered email address.
Open the email and select Confirm subscription to complete the registration process.
After successful confirmation, the subscription status will change to Confirmed.
4. Test Notification Sending On the Topic interface, select Publish message.
Enter the following information for testing:
Subject: Dental Clinic Notification Message: Test notification from Dental Clinic Management System. Then select Publish message.
If the configuration is successful, your registered email address will receive notifications from Amazon SNS.
At this point, Amazon SNS is ready to integrate with the Backend to send notifications from the Dental Clinic Management System.

title: Event 2
content:
EVENT REPORT “SATURDAY MEET UP - JUNE 06, 2026” Event Objectives Learn practical applications of AWS in different areas such as gaming, system administration, data, DevOps, and cybersecurity. Expand cloud computing knowledge through topics such as WebSocket, Serverless, Graph Database, Docker, and AWS WAF. Listen to career experience from IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin and the transition toward Cloud/DevOps. Understand the role of teamwork in learning, project development, and product building. Learn how AI and Machine Learning can be applied in GraphRAG and cyber attack detection. Topics and Speakers No. Topic Speaker Main Content 1 Multiplayer in the Cloud: Connecting Godot Clients with AWS WebSockets Nguyễn Quốc Bảo Building a multiplayer game using Godot with AWS WebSocket, Lambda, and DynamoDB. 2 From IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin Vinh Trần Sharing the career path from IT Helpdesk to Sysadmin and the transition to Cloud/DevOps. 3 The Art of Effective Teamwork Trương Phước Presenting principles for effective teamwork. 4 AWS Neptune for Building a Graph Knowledge Base for GraphRAG Việt Phát Introducing GraphRAG, Amazon Neptune, and graph-based knowledge bases. 5 Docker - A Containerization Technology Bảo Huỳnh Explaining Docker, containerization, and the role of Docker in software development. 6 Combining AWS WAF with Machine Learning for Cyber Attack Detection on AWS Lê Hoàng Gia Đại Combining AWS WAF and Machine Learning to detect cyber attacks on AWS. Key Highlights Multiplayer in the Cloud: Connecting Godot Clients with AWS WebSockets The session introduced how to build a simple multiplayer system on the cloud. Godot clients can connect to the backend through WebSocket. AWS API Gateway WebSocket is used to maintain connections between clients and the backend. AWS Lambda handles logic such as player connection, matchmaking, and game results. DynamoDB is used to store player states such as connection ID, waiting status, and opponent information. The content helped participants understand how AWS can support basic realtime applications. From IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin The session focused on the career development path in IT Infrastructure. IT Helpdesk is a good foundation for developing troubleshooting skills and user communication. To become a Sysadmin, learners need to build knowledge in Linux, Networking, Servers, Monitoring, and Security. When transitioning to Cloud/DevOps, learners need to become familiar with automation, Infrastructure as Code, CI/CD, Docker, and cloud platforms. An important lesson is not to learn too many topics at the same time, but to build a strong foundation first. The Art of Effective Teamwork Effective teamwork requires clear goals that are shared among all members. Each member should be assigned to the right role based on skills and strengths. Open communication, active listening, and clear feedback help reduce misunderstandings during teamwork. Personal accountability is important to maintain overall team progress. Some useful tools for teamwork include Trello, ClickUp, Google Workspace, Slack, and Discord. AWS Neptune for Building a Graph Knowledge Base for GraphRAG The session introduced GraphRAG, an extension of Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Traditional RAG helps LLMs retrieve information from external sources but may be limited when multi-hop reasoning is required. GraphRAG uses a graph database to represent relationships between entities. Amazon Neptune can be used to build a graph knowledge base. GraphRAG is suitable for use cases that require understanding complex relationships, such as intelligent Q&A systems or enterprise knowledge bases. The topic also mentioned combining Amazon Bedrock, Neptune Analytics, and data processing pipelines to support smarter retrieval. Docker - A Containerization Technology Docker packages applications together with the runtime environment and required dependencies. Containers are lighter than virtual machines because each application does not need a separate operating system. Docker supports the idea of “build once, run anywhere”. Important Docker concepts include Docker Image, Docker Container, Dockerfile, Layer, and Docker commands. Docker is commonly used in CI/CD, microservices, development/testing environments, and cloud-native applications. The session helped explain why Docker is an important foundation for Cloud and DevOps. Combining AWS WAF with Machine Learning for Cyber Attack Detection on AWS AWS WAF helps protect web applications from common attacks such as SQL Injection, XSS, bot traffic, and abnormal requests. Rule-based WAF protection is effective for known attack patterns but may be limited against new types of attacks. Machine Learning can help detect abnormal behavior based on network data. The session mentioned dataset processing, data cleaning, model training, and result evaluation. The AWS architecture may combine services such as WAF, EC2, ALB, S3, Lambda, CloudWatch, GuardDuty, Security Hub, and SNS. The content helped participants understand the combination of Cloud Security and Machine Learning. What I Learned AWS Cloud AWS is not only used for creating servers or storing data, but can also support realtime games, AI, security, and distributed systems. Services such as API Gateway, Lambda, and DynamoDB can be combined to create a simple serverless backend. Amazon Neptune supports graph-based data storage, which is suitable for relationship-heavy problems. AWS WAF, CloudWatch, GuardDuty, and Security Hub are important services when building security-aware systems. DevOps and Systems Docker is an important tool that helps applications run consistently across different environments. To follow a Cloud/DevOps path, learners need foundations in Linux, Networking, System Administration, Automation, and CI/CD. Learning cloud effectively requires a combination of theory, hands-on labs, and real projects. When deploying systems, monitoring, security, cost, and scalability should be considered. AI and Data GraphRAG helps LLMs better handle questions that require understanding relationships between multiple entities. Machine Learning can support cyber attack detection by identifying abnormal behaviors. Data quality is very important in AI/ML problems. AI should not be treated as a standalone tool, but should be combined with data, infrastructure, and proper control processes. Teamwork and Career Direction Effective teamwork requires shared goals, clear task assignment, and personal accountability. Communication and listening skills are as important as technical skills. The path from IT Helpdesk to Sysadmin and Cloud/DevOps requires time, practice, and a strong foundation. A practical portfolio and real projects are valuable for career development. Application to Work When learning AWS, I can try combining several small services to understand how a cloud system works end-to-end. Docker can be applied to package personal projects, making them easier to deploy and test. In team projects, tasks should be clearly assigned, progress should be updated regularly, and goals should be aligned from the beginning. For AI-related projects, it is important to pay attention to data sources, data organization, and result evaluation. When learning cloud security, multiple protection layers such as WAF, logging, monitoring, and alerting should be considered. Knowledge of Sysadmin, DevOps, and Cloud can support my future career direction after graduation. Event Experience The event on June 6 provided a wide range of topics, not focusing on only one AWS service but expanding into realtime games, DevOps, AI, GraphRAG, Docker, and Cybersecurity. The sessions helped me see more clearly how AWS can be applied in real situations, from game backend development and knowledge base construction to cyber attack detection.
One useful point was that the topics were closely related to the learning path of IT students. For example, the session from IT Helpdesk to Sysadmin helped me understand the foundation needed before learning Cloud/DevOps. The Docker session helped me realize that containerization is an important skill for modern application deployment. The GraphRAG and AWS WAF with Machine Learning sessions also expanded my view of how AI can be applied in data and security.
Networking and Discussion Had the opportunity to access multiple technical topics in one event. Learned practical experience and career direction from the speakers. Gained ideas that can be applied to AWS learning, personal projects, and Cloud/DevOps preparation. Realized that technical skills should go together with teamwork, communication, and problem-solving mindset. Key Takeaways AWS is a broad ecosystem that can support many types of applications, from web, games, and data to AI and security. To learn Cloud effectively, it is important to understand fundamentals such as networking, systems, databases, security, and deployment. Docker is an important skill for Cloud Native and DevOps directions. AI/ML can create significant value when combined properly with data and cloud infrastructure. Security should not rely only on fixed rules, but also needs monitoring, logging, and smarter detection methods. Good teamwork helps projects run more effectively and reduces risks during development. Some Images from the Event

title: Configure Access Permissions
content:
In the Dental Clinic Management System, securing image data (such as personal photos of doctors and patients) is crucial. Therefore, the system does not allow public access but uses a dynamic access granting mechanism via the backend.
1. Bucket Security Policy In the Bucket creation step, we kept the default setting of Block all public access.
This means that no one on the internet can directly view or download images from Amazon S3 using the original URL. Attempts to access will result in Amazon S3 blocking and returning an Access Denied error.
Only the backend application (via the IAM User dental-backend-user with AmazonS3FullAccess permissions) can legitimately communicate with, read, and write files to this Bucket.
2. Granting Display Permissions Using Pre-signed URLs To enable the Frontend (ReactJS) to display images to the user, the Spring Boot Backend acts as an intermediary, granting permissions through the Pre-signed URL mechanism (a digitally signed URL for authentication).
In the Backend source code, the AwsConfig class has initialized the S3Presigner Bean:
@Bean public S3Presigner s3Presigner(AwsCredentialsProvider credentialsProvider) { return S3Presigner.builder() .region(Region.of(region)) .credentialsProvider(credentialsProvider) .build(); }

title: Proposal
content:
Dental Clinic Management System Dental Clinic Appointment and Management Website on AWS Cloud 1. Executive Summary The Dental Clinic System is a web-based application that enables patients to book dental appointments online, browse available services, track appointment status, and receive notifications from the clinic. The system also assists dentists and administrators in managing appointments, dental services, patient records, and daily clinic operations.
The system is built on a Cloud Native Architecture using AWS services to provide high availability, security, scalability, and performance. The frontend is deployed using AWS Amplify, while the backend is developed with Spring Boot and hosted on Amazon EC2. Application data is stored in Amazon DynamoDB, medical images and uploaded files are stored in Amazon S3, and services such as CloudWatch, SNS, SES, AWS WAF, IAM, Secrets Manager, and AWS KMS are integrated to ensure reliable and secure operation.
2. Problem Statement Current Problems Many dental clinics still manage appointments manually through phone calls or paper-based records, which leads to several issues:
Difficult appointment management. Appointment conflicts and scheduling errors. Inefficient patient information management. No automatic notification system. Limited scalability as the number of patients increases. Proposed Solution The Dental Clinic System provides a cloud-based appointment management platform running on AWS Cloud.
Patients can:
Register and log in. Book dental appointments. View appointment history. Cancel appointments. Browse available dental services. Track appointment status. Dentists can:
View their schedules. Manage patient appointments. Update treatment status. Administrators can:
Manage users. Manage dentists. Manage dental services. Manage appointments. Monitor overall system activities. Benefits Reduce appointment processing time. Automate appointment scheduling. Improve patient experience. Provide a scalable cloud infrastructure. Enhance data security. Reduce operational costs by utilizing AWS Cloud services. 3. Solution Architecture The system follows a Cloud Native Architecture on AWS.
The frontend is deployed using AWS Amplify, together with Amazon Route 53, Amazon CloudFront, and AWS WAF to improve performance and protect the application.
The backend is deployed on Amazon EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to handle incoming requests efficiently.
Application data is stored in Amazon DynamoDB, while images and uploaded files are stored in Amazon S3.
Sensitive configuration data is securely managed using AWS Secrets Manager and encrypted with AWS KMS.
Amazon CloudWatch monitors system resources, while Amazon SNS and Amazon SES deliver alerts and appointment confirmation emails.
AWS Services Used Amazon Route 53: Manages domain names and routes users to the application using DNS. Amazon CloudFront: Delivers content globally with low latency through a Content Delivery Network (CDN). AWS WAF: Protects the application against common web attacks such as SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). AWS Amplify: Hosts and deploys the React frontend with automatic build and deployment from GitHub. Application Load Balancer (ALB): Distributes incoming traffic across backend services to improve availability. Amazon EC2: Hosts the Spring Boot backend application and processes business logic. Amazon DynamoDB: Stores users, dentists, services, appointments, and other application data. Amazon S3: Stores doctor images, service images, user uploads, and static files. AWS Secrets Manager: Securely stores sensitive credentials such as JWT secrets and API keys. AWS Key Management Service (KMS): Encrypts sensitive data and manages encryption keys. Amazon CloudWatch: Collects logs and monitors application performance. Amazon SNS: Sends system notifications and operational alerts. Amazon SES: Sends appointment confirmations, reminders, and email notifications. AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM): Controls user permissions and access to AWS resources. Component Design Frontend
ReactJS AWS Amplify Amazon CloudFront Amazon Route 53 AWS WAF Backend
Spring Boot Amazon EC2 Application Load Balancer Database
Amazon DynamoDB Storage
Amazon S3 Security
AWS IAM AWS Secrets Manager AWS KMS Monitoring
Amazon CloudWatch Amazon SNS Amazon SES 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases The project is divided into four main phases, from requirement analysis to deployment on AWS Cloud.
Requirement Analysis and System Design: Analyze the requirements of the dental clinic, identify core features such as user management, dentist management, appointment scheduling, and service management, then design the database, user interface, and AWS Cloud architecture.
System Development: Develop the frontend using ReactJS and the backend using Java Spring Boot following the RESTful API architecture. Configure Amazon DynamoDB for data storage and Amazon S3 for image storage while implementing all business functionalities.
AWS Deployment: Deploy the frontend using AWS Amplify and the backend on Amazon EC2. Configure Application Load Balancer (ALB), Amazon Route 53, Amazon CloudFront, and AWS WAF to improve performance, availability, and security.
Testing, Optimization, and Operation: Perform functional testing, security testing, and performance testing. Monitor the system using Amazon CloudWatch, configure Amazon SNS and Amazon SES for notifications and email confirmations, and optimize both cost and system performance before production deployment.
Technical Requirements Frontend
ReactJS with Tailwind CSS. Axios for REST API communication. AWS Amplify deployment. Amazon CloudFront for CDN. Amazon Route 53 for DNS management. AWS WAF for application protection. Backend
Java Spring Boot. RESTful API architecture. JWT Authentication. Amazon EC2 deployment. Application Load Balancer. AWS Secrets Manager. AWS KMS. Database and Storage
Amazon DynamoDB for application data. Amazon S3 for storing images and uploaded files. Monitoring and Notification
Amazon CloudWatch for monitoring logs and resources. Amazon SNS for operational notifications. Amazon SES for appointment confirmation and reminder emails. Security
AWS IAM for access control. AWS Secrets Manager for credential management. AWS KMS for data encryption. Deployment Environment
Frontend: ReactJS + AWS Amplify. Backend: Java Spring Boot on Amazon EC2. Database: Amazon DynamoDB. Storage: Amazon S3. Domain: Amazon Route 53. CDN: Amazon CloudFront. Security: AWS WAF, IAM, Secrets Manager, AWS KMS. Monitoring: Amazon CloudWatch. Notification: Amazon SNS and Amazon SES. 5. Project Timeline Month 1 Requirement analysis. UI/UX design. Database design. AWS architecture design. Month 2 Backend development. Frontend development. DynamoDB integration. Amazon S3 integration. Month 3 AWS deployment. System testing. Performance optimization. Final documentation. 6. Budget Estimation Infrastructure costs are estimated using the AWS Pricing Calculator.
Infrastructure Cost Amazon EC2: 0.30 USD/month Application Load Balancer: 0.06 USD/month Amazon DynamoDB: 0.08 USD/month Amazon S3: 0.10 USD/month AWS Amplify: 0.15 USD/month Amazon CloudFront: 0.05 USD/month Amazon Route 53: 0.04 USD/month AWS WAF: 0.10 USD/month Amazon CloudWatch: 0.05 USD/month Amazon SNS: 0.02 USD/month Amazon SES: 0.04 USD/month AWS Secrets Manager: 0.03 USD/month AWS KMS: 0.02 USD/month Total: 1.04 USD/month, approximately 12.48 USD/year (estimated for a small-scale demonstration environment using AWS Free Tier whenever applicable).
7. Risk Assessment Risk Matrix Amazon EC2 failure. Unexpected traffic spikes. Internet connectivity issues. AWS configuration errors. Credential leakage. Mitigation Strategy Monitor the system with Amazon CloudWatch. Protect the application using AWS WAF. Store sensitive credentials in AWS Secrets Manager. Apply IAM role-based access control. Perform regular data backups. Contingency Plan Restore uploaded files from Amazon S3. Redeploy the backend on Amazon EC2. Analyze logs through Amazon CloudWatch. Notify administrators via Amazon SNS and Amazon SES. 8. Expected Outcomes Successfully develop an online dental appointment management system. Deploy a stable and secure application on AWS Cloud. Improve appointment management efficiency. Enhance patient experience. Provide a scalable cloud architecture for future expansion. Support future features such as online payment, AI chatbot, and Electronic Medical Records (EMR).

title: Event 3
content:
EVENT REPORT “FCAJ COMMUNITY DAY” - EVENT 3, June 27, 2026 Event Objectives Learn how AI is applied in modern cloud operations. Understand the shift from traditional alert-based systems to autonomous response and resolution systems. Explore the role of Voice Agents in building natural human-like AI conversations. Learn about AWS DevOps Agent and how AI can help reduce detection and resolution time in cloud environments. Understand how AI supports enterprise productivity, especially in workforce planning and data-driven decision-making. Learn about Amazon Quick, MCP, and secure private connections for enterprise AI assistant systems. Event Agenda Time Topic Main Content 08:30 - 09:00 Check-in Settle into the seat and prepare for the event. 09:00 - 09:25 Deep Response Engine: From Detection to Autonomous Resolution Discussed complexity in modern cloud operations, the shift from alert-driven to action-driven systems, Deep Response Engine architecture, autonomous incident response demo, and business impact such as cost reduction and zero-downtime operations. 09:25 - 09:55 Voice Agents: Building Human-Like AI Conversations at Scale Covered the evolution from IVR and chatbots to AI voice agents, challenges in latency, accuracy, and natural interaction, Amazon Nova Sonic, speech-to-speech foundation model, and voice agent architecture. 09:55 - 10:20 AWS DevOps Agent: Your Always-Available Operations Teammate Introduced AWS DevOps Agent, reducing MTTD and MTTR with AI-driven operations, supporting multi-cloud and hybrid environments, Bedrock AgentCore, multi-agent reasoning, and ECS demo walkthrough. 10:20 - 10:45 AI-Powered Productivity: Workforce Planning For Enterprise Presented HR transformation challenges, Amazon Quick and its HR capabilities, HR automation, workforce analytics, and strategic workforce planning for enterprise decision-making. 10:45 - 11:30 Building Secure Private MCP Connection with Amazon Quick Introduced Amazon Quick as an AI assistant platform, MCP extensibility, security challenges in MCP-based integrations, Amazon Quick VPC private connectivity, demo, and real-world implementation insights. Key Highlights Deep Response Engine: From Detection to Autonomous Resolution The session focused on the increasing complexity of operating modern cloud systems. As the number of services, logs, metrics, and alerts grows, operations teams may struggle to identify root causes and resolve incidents quickly. Deep Response Engine aims to move from simply receiving alerts to recommending or performing response actions. This architecture helps systems respond faster to incidents, reduce downtime, and lower operational costs. The demo helped participants visualize how a system can automatically detect and respond to incidents. Voice Agents: Building Human-Like AI Conversations at Scale The session introduced the evolution from traditional IVR and text-based chatbots to AI voice agents. Voice agents allow users to interact with systems through more natural speech. Key challenges include latency, speech recognition accuracy, and human-like interaction. Amazon Nova Sonic and speech-to-speech foundation models were mentioned as technologies that support real-time conversational experiences. A voice agent architecture may combine telephony, streaming, Amazon Bedrock, and MCP tools to create intelligent communication systems at scale. AWS DevOps Agent: Your Always-Available Operations Teammate AWS DevOps Agent was introduced as an always-available operations assistant for cloud and DevOps teams. AI can help reduce MTTD and MTTR by detecting, analyzing, and suggesting incident resolution steps faster. The session also discussed support for multi-cloud and hybrid environments, which is useful for enterprises with complex infrastructure. Bedrock AgentCore and multi-agent reasoning allow agents to collaborate on different operational tasks. The ECS demo helped participants understand how an AI agent can assist in operating container workloads. AI-Powered Productivity: Workforce Planning For Enterprise The session focused on how AI can improve productivity in enterprise environments. In HR, enterprises often face challenges related to workforce data, manual processes, and limited insights for decision-making. Amazon Quick can support task automation, workforce data analysis, and insight generation for planning. Workforce analytics helps enterprises understand staffing needs, hiring requirements, and resource allocation. AI can support strategic workforce planning by providing data-driven insights for enterprise decisions. Building Secure Private MCP Connection with Amazon Quick The session introduced Amazon Quick as an AI assistant platform that can be extended through tools and external connections. MCP (Model Context Protocol) helps AI assistants connect with data, tools, or other systems more flexibly. When integrating MCP in enterprise environments, security is important because the system may access internal data. VPC private connectivity helps establish private connections and reduces the need for data to travel through the public Internet. This topic helped participants understand that enterprise AI assistant deployment requires not only intelligence but also proper security design. What I Learned AI in Cloud Operations Understood that AI can support cloud and DevOps teams in detecting and resolving incidents. Learned the difference between alert-driven systems and action-driven systems. Realized that reducing MTTD and MTTR is an important goal in cloud operations. Understood that AI agents can act as operations teammates that analyze logs, metrics, and suggest actions. Voice Agents and User Experience Understood the evolution from IVR and chatbots to AI voice agents. Learned that building voice agents at scale requires solving latency, accuracy, and natural interaction challenges. Gained an initial understanding of Amazon Nova Sonic and speech-to-speech foundation models. Realized that voice agents can be applied in customer support, call centers, internal assistance, and enterprise workflows. Enterprise Productivity Understood how AI can support automation in enterprise processes. Learned how workforce analytics helps enterprises make data-driven decisions. Realized that AI is not only useful for technical tasks but can also support HR, operations, and business management. Security in AI Integration Understood that when AI assistants connect to internal data and tools, security must be prioritized. Learned the role of MCP in extending AI assistant capabilities. Became aware of the importance of private connectivity and VPC when deploying AI in enterprise environments. Understood that enterprise AI systems need to balance scalability, convenience, and risk control. Application to Work When learning AWS, I can pay more attention to services related to operations, monitoring, and incident automation. In personal or team projects, I can apply an action-driven mindset instead of only recording errors manually. When using AI assistants, I should provide clear context and consider how AI connects to external data. When designing AI-based systems, security, access control, and data flow should be considered. For the Cloud/DevOps direction, I need to learn more about incident response, observability, ECS, agent-based automation, and multi-agent systems. For the BA/DA or Information Systems direction, AI can be applied in data analysis, dashboards, workforce planning, and decision support. Event Experience The event provided a practical view of how AI is being integrated deeply into cloud operations and enterprise workflows. The topics did not only discuss AI for content generation or simple chatbots, but also expanded into incident response systems, DevOps support, voice agents, and secure connections to internal enterprise data.
One useful point was that the topics were closely related to real enterprise implementation. For example, Deep Response Engine and AWS DevOps Agent showed how AI can reduce incident resolution time. Voice Agents showed how AI can improve communication experiences. Amazon Quick and MCP helped me understand how AI assistants can connect with data, workflows, and internal systems more securely.
Networking and Discussion Had the opportunity to learn about new trends in AI-driven operations, voice agents, and enterprise AI. Understood more about how AWS services can support system operations and enterprise productivity. Gained ideas that can be applied to AWS, AI, DevOps learning, and personal projects. Realized that enterprise AI deployment requires a combination of technology, data, security, and business value. Key Takeaways AI can support not only content generation but also system operations, incident response, and decision-making. Modern cloud systems need to move from simple alerting to smarter analysis and action. Voice Agents are an important direction in AI-powered user experience. AWS DevOps Agent and multi-agent reasoning open new possibilities for cloud operations automation. When AI assistants connect to enterprise data, security and private connectivity are very important. The event gave me more motivation to continue learning AWS, AI, DevOps, and how these technologies are applied in real environments. Some Images from the Event Some Images from the Event

title: Configure SNS Notifications
content:
In this section, we will configure Amazon SNS (Simple Notification Service) to automatically send notification emails (e.g., system alerts, reports) to your Gmail address.
1. Creating an SNS Topic Access the AWS Management Console, search for Amazon SNS, and select Topics. Click the Create topic button. In the Type section, select Standard. Enter a Name (e.g., dental-system-alerts). Keep the default settings and click Create topic. 2. Subscribing to Gmail for notifications After the topic is created, we need to add the email address that will receive notifications:
In the details interface of the newly created topic, switch to the Subscriptions tab and click Create subscription. In the Protocol field, select Email. In the Endpoint field, enter your Gmail address (e.g., your-email@gmail.com). Click Create subscription. 3. Confirm Subscription AWS SNS will send a confirmation email to the Gmail address you just entered. Open your Gmail inbox and find the email with the subject AWS Notification - Subscription Confirmation. Click the Confirm subscription link in the email. Return to the AWS Console and check that the subscription status changes from Pending confirmation to Confirmed. 4. Publish Message On the Topic interface in AWS, click the Publish message button. Enter the Subject (e.g., Test Alert from Dental System). Enter the message in the Message body to send to the endpoint field (e.g., The system is working normally!). Scroll to the bottom and click Publish message. Check your Gmail inbox; you will receive the message you just sent. The SNS system is now ready to integrate with CloudWatch or the backend to automatically send notifications.

title: Events Participated

title: Configure Amazon S3
content:
In the Dental Clinic Management System, Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is used to store system image files such as doctor photos, service photos, and user-uploaded files.
1. Creating an S3 Bucket Log in to the AWS Management Console, search for Amazon S3, and select Buckets.
In the Amazon S3 interface, select Create bucket to create a new bucket.
Set up the basic information:
Bucket name: dental-service-images-huy AWS Region: Asia Pacific (Singapore) - ap-southeast-1 After completing the configuration, select Create bucket.
After successful creation, the dental-service-images-huy Bucket will appear in the Buckets list and be ready for use.
2. Configuring Amazon S3 in the Backend After creating the Bucket, configure the Bucket information in the application.yml file of the Backend.
aws: region: ${AWS_REGION} s3: bucket-name: ${AWS_S3_BUCKET_NAME} Where:
AWS_REGION: The region where the AWS service is deployed.
AWS_S3_BUCKET_NAME: The name of the Amazon S3 Bucket (dental-service-images-huy).
The backend will use this information to connect to Amazon S3 and perform image upload, download, and management operations within the system.
After completing the configuration and launching the application, the backend can store and retrieve images directly from Amazon S3.

title: Configure Amazon SES & SNS
content:
In the Dental Clinic Management System, Amazon SES is used to send appointment confirmation emails and notifications to users, while Amazon SNS is used to manage notifications via the Publish/Subscribe model.
1. Configuring Amazon SES Log in to the AWS Management Console, search for Amazon SES, and select Configuration → Identities.
Select Create identity, then select Email address and enter the email address that will be used to send notifications from the system.
After successful creation, AWS will send a verification email to the registered address. Open the email and select Verify email address to complete the verification process.
2. Configuring Amazon SNS Log in to the AWS Management Console, search for Amazon SNS, and select Topics.
Select Create topic, choose the Standard type, and name the topic:
dental-clinic-notification Then click Create topic to create the topic.
After successful creation, the system will generate a Topic ARN, which is used by the backend to send notifications to Amazon SNS.
3. Backend Configuration Update the application.yml file to declare the necessary information:
aws: region: ${AWS_REGION} ses: sender-email: ${AWS_SES_SENDER_EMAIL} sns: topic-arn: ${AWS_SNS_TOPIC_ARN} Where:
AWS_SES_SENDER_EMAIL: The email address verified on Amazon SES. AWS_SNS_TOPIC_ARN: The ARN of the Topic created on Amazon SNS. After completing the configuration and restarting the Backend, the system is ready to send emails via Amazon SES and broadcast notifications via Amazon SNS.

title: Workshop
content:
Deploying the Dental Clinic Management Website on AWS Overview In this workshop, I will demonstrate the deployment process of the Dental Clinic Management Website on the Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud platform.
The project is designed to address the limitations of traditional dental clinic management, including:
Managing appointments manually using paper or spreadsheets, which can easily lead to scheduling conflicts and data loss. Requiring patients to call or visit the clinic directly to book appointments, resulting in inconvenience for both patients and clinic staff. Difficulty in managing dentists, services, appointments, and patient information within a centralized system. Limited capability to monitor system performance and application health as the number of users increases. Challenges in scaling and maintaining applications deployed on traditional on-premises infrastructure. To overcome these challenges, the system is built using a 3-Tier Architecture, with ReactJS as the Frontend, Spring Boot as the Backend, and Amazon DynamoDB as the NoSQL database. Deploying the application on AWS provides improved scalability, high availability, enhanced security, and simplified infrastructure management.
Throughout this workshop, readers will learn how to deploy the application step by step on AWS, including preparing the cloud environment, deploying the Frontend and Backend, configuring database and storage services, implementing security features, monitoring system performance, testing the application, and cleaning up AWS resources after deployment.
The AWS services used in this workshop include:
Amazon EC2 AWS Amplify Amazon DynamoDB Amazon S3 Amazon CloudFront Amazon Route 53 AWS WAF AWS Secrets Manager AWS Key Management Service (KMS) Amazon CloudWatch Amazon SNS Amazon SES AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) Contents Introduction Prerequisites Configure DynamoDB Configure Amazon S3 Configure Amazon SES & SNS Deploy Backend on EC2 System Testing Resource Cleanup

title: Deploy Backend on EC2
content:
In this section, we will deploy the Spring Boot Backend of the Dental Clinic Management System to Amazon EC2. After successful deployment, the Backend will connect to AWS services such as Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon S3, Amazon SES, and Amazon SNS to provide full system functionality.
5.6.1. Creating an EC2 Instance Log in to the AWS Management Console, search for Amazon EC2, and select Launch Instance.
Configure the EC2 as follows:
Name: Dental-Backend Amazon Machine Image (AMI): Amazon Linux 2023 Instance type: t3.micro Key pair: Use or create a new Key Pair to connect via SSH. Security Group: SSH (22) HTTP (80) Custom TCP (8080) After completing the configuration, press Launch Instance to create the EC2 server.
5.6.2. Connecting to EC2 After EC2 enters Running mode, use EC2 Instance Connect or SSH to connect to the server.
Example:
ssh -i keydental.pem ec2-user@<Public-IP> After a successful connection, update the system and install Docker to prepare for application deployment.
5.6.3. Deploying the Backend Upload the backend source code or Docker image to EC2.
Next, start the application using Docker:
docker build -t dental-backend . docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --env-file .env dental-backend After the application starts successfully, the backend will automatically connect to:
Amazon DynamoDB Amazon S3 Amazon SES Amazon SNS to handle data storage, image management, and email notifications.
5.6.4. Testing the System After deployment is complete, access the backend via the EC2 public IP address:
http://<Public-IP>:8080 Or use Postman to test the system APIs.
If the backend responds successfully and can access data from Amazon DynamoDB, upload images to Amazon S3, and send emails via Amazon SES and Amazon SNS, then the deployment is complete.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
During my participation in the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program, I had the opportunity to gain valuable knowledge, improve my technical skills, and experience a professional learning environment. Below are my personal thoughts and feedback based on my internship experience.
Overall Evaluation 1. Learning Environment The learning environment was professional, friendly, and highly supportive. Throughout the program, mentors and community members were willing to answer questions and share their practical experiences. The weekly learning roadmap was well organized, making it easier to follow and complete each milestone.
2. Mentor and Community Support The mentors provided clear explanations and practical guidance whenever I encountered difficulties. Besides the official learning materials, I also received useful advice from other participants through the community channels, which helped me solve problems more efficiently.
3. Relevance to My Major The program matched well with my Information Technology background. Topics such as AWS Cloud, serverless architecture, monitoring, security, and Generative AI complemented what I had learned at university while introducing many modern cloud technologies used in real-world environments.
4. Learning Opportunities and Skill Development Throughout the internship, I improved both my technical and soft skills. I gained hands-on experience with AWS services, Hugo, Markdown, GitHub, documentation writing, and presentation skills. Participating in workshops and community events also broadened my understanding of current cloud and AI trends.
5. Community Culture One of the most impressive aspects of FCAJ was its collaborative culture. Participants were encouraged to share knowledge, discuss solutions, and support one another. This positive environment made learning more enjoyable and motivated me to keep improving.
6. Program Organization The program schedule, assignments, and learning materials were well prepared. Weekly objectives were clearly defined, allowing participants to monitor their own progress. Community events and technical workshops also provided valuable opportunities to connect with industry professionals.
Additional Questions What was the most satisfying part of your internship? The most rewarding experience was being able to apply cloud technologies in practical tasks instead of only learning theory. Building weekly worklogs, attending technical workshops, and interacting with AWS professionals significantly improved my confidence and technical knowledge.
What could be improved for future participants? It would be helpful to provide additional hands-on practice sessions or mini projects after each major topic so participants can reinforce what they have learned through real implementation.
Would you recommend this program to your friends? Yes. I would definitely recommend FCAJ to students who want to begin their cloud computing journey. The program offers a structured learning path, experienced mentors, practical workshops, and an active learning community that creates an excellent environment for skill development.
Suggestions and Future Expectations Continue organizing more technical workshops focusing on real-world cloud solutions and AI applications. Include more collaborative mini projects to encourage teamwork and practical experience. Expand opportunities for networking with AWS experts and industry professionals. I would be happy to participate in future FCAJ activities and advanced learning programs if given the opportunity. Overall, participating in the First Cloud AI Journey program has been a valuable experience. It strengthened my technical foundation, improved my professional skills, and gave me greater confidence in pursuing a career in Cloud Computing and Artificial Intelligence.

title: System Testing
content:
After completing the deployment of the Dental Clinic Management System to AWS, the final step is to test the entire system to ensure that the services are stable and can communicate with each other.
In this section, we will verify that the Amazon EC2 Backend has successfully connected to Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon S3, Amazon SES, and Amazon SNS, and test the main functions of the system.
5.7.1. Checking EC2 Status First, access the Amazon EC2 Console and check the status of the Instance.
If the Instance shows a Running status and passes all Status Checks, this indicates that the server is ready to run the Backend application.
5.7.2. Checking Data Storage Log in to the system and create or update data.
Then access Amazon DynamoDB to confirm that the data has been successfully saved to the system tables.
5.7.3. Checking Image Upload Upload a profile picture or service image from the system interface.
After a successful upload, access Amazon S3 and check the Bucket to confirm that the image file has been saved.
5.7.4. Checking Email Sending Schedule an appointment or use the email sending function from the system.
If the user receives a notification email in Gmail, it proves that Amazon SES has been configured and is functioning correctly.
After completing the above checks, the Dental Clinic Management System has been successfully deployed on the AWS platform. The backend is stable on Amazon EC2 and can connect to Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon S3, Amazon SES, and Amazon SNS to fully support the system’s functions.

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
After completing the Workshop and successfully verifying the system’s functionality, the final step is to clean up the resources created on AWS to avoid unnecessary costs.
In this section, we will sequentially delete the resources used during the deployment of the Dental Clinic Management System.
5.8.1. Deleting an EC2 Instance Access Amazon EC2. Select Instances. Select the created EC2 Instance. Select Instance state → Terminate instance. Confirm the deletion. 5.8.2. Deleting an Amazon S3 Bucket Access Amazon S3. Select the project’s Bucket. Delete all objects in the Bucket. Then select Delete bucket. Enter the Bucket name to confirm and complete the deletion. Note: In this Workshop, the team did not delete the Amazon S3 Bucket because the Bucket is still used to store system images and for development and testing in subsequent phases.
5.8.3. Deleting Amazon DynamoDB Tables Access Amazon DynamoDB. Select Tables. Select the project tables such as: users doctors services appointments invoices feedbacks consultations clinics Select Delete table. Enter confirmation and complete the deletion. Note: In this workshop, the team did not delete the Amazon DynamoDB tables because the data is still being used for system development, testing, and demos.
5.8.4. Deleting Amazon SES Identity Access Amazon SES. Select Configuration → Identities. Select the verified Email Identity. Select Delete to delete the Identity. Note: In this workshop, the team did not delete the Email Identity on Amazon SES because it is still being used to send appointment confirmation emails and system notifications.
5.8.5. Deleting Amazon SES Identity Access Amazon SES. Select Configuration → Identities. Select the verified Email Identity. Select Delete to delete the Identity. > Note: In this workshop, the team did not delete the Email Identity on Amazon SES as it will continue to be used to send appointment confirmation emails and system notifications. After completing the above steps, all resources used in the workshop have been cleaned up. This helps avoid additional costs and keeps the AWS account clean and ready for future deployments.

title: Week 9 Worklog
content:
Week 9 Objectives: Learn about Elastic Load Balancing and the role of Load Balancer in AWS systems. Understand the concepts of Target Group, Listener, and Health Check. Learn about Auto Scaling Group and how AWS automatically adjusts the number of EC2 Instances. Become familiar with Launch Template and basic scaling policies. Minimum Capacity Desired Capacity Maximum Capacity Understood that Launch Template is used to define the EC2 Instance configuration when Auto Scaling needs to launch new instances.

title: Week 10 Worklog
content:
Week 10 Objectives: Learn about Serverless Computing on AWS. Become familiar with AWS Lambda and how to run code without managing servers. Understand basic Lambda components such as Function, Runtime, Handler, Trigger, and Execution Role. Learn about Amazon API Gateway and how API Gateway can connect with Lambda. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Monday - Learn the overview of Serverless Computing - Understand the difference between EC2 and Lambda - Take notes on common AWS Lambda use cases 29/06/2026 29/06/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Tuesday - Explore the AWS Lambda interface on AWS Console - Learn about Function, Runtime, Handler, and Execution Role - Observe the steps to create a basic Lambda Function 30/06/2026 30/06/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/ Wednesday - Practice creating a simple Lambda Function - Test the result using a Test Event - View Lambda execution logs in CloudWatch Logs 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/getting-started.html Thursday - Learn about Amazon API Gateway - Understand REST API, Resource, Method, and Endpoint - Learn how API Gateway can invoke a Lambda Function 02/07/2026 02/07/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/ Friday - Summarize the basic model: Client → API Gateway → Lambda → CloudWatch Logs - Review the resources created during the week - Delete unnecessary resources after practice - Update Week 10 Worklog on the personal Hugo website 03/07/2026 03/07/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/welcome.html Week 10 Achievements: Understood that Serverless Computing allows applications to run without directly managing servers.

EC2 requires managing virtual servers Lambda runs code based on events while AWS manages the underlying infrastructure Understood the role of AWS Lambda in handling small tasks, automation, and event-driven processing.
